cards.definition: Not willing to do something; hesitant
cards.definitionSimple: When you really don't want to do something
cards.exampleSentence: She was reluctant to leave the party early.

cards.definition: Intensely irritated and frustrated
cards.definitionSimple: Super annoyed and fed up
cards.exampleSentence: The teacher was exasperated by the constant noise.

cards.definition: Appearing casually calm and relaxed
cards.definitionSimple: Acting cool like nothing bothers you
cards.exampleSentence: He seemed nonchalant about the exam results.

cards.definition: Feeling or showing sympathy and concern for others
cards.definitionSimple: Caring deeply about other people's feelings
cards.exampleSentence: The compassionate nurse stayed with the patient all night.

cards.definition: Treating someone unfairly to benefit yourself
cards.definitionSimple: Using people in an unfair way to get what you want
cards.exampleSentence: The exploitative landlord charged too much rent.

cards.definition: Respected and admired; having high status
cards.definitionSimple: Famous and respected by everyone
cards.exampleSentence: She won a place at a prestigious school.

cards.definition: Full of danger or risk
cards.definitionSimple: Very dangerous
cards.exampleSentence: The mountaineers faced a perilous climb.

cards.definition: Noisy, energetic, and cheerful
cards.definitionSimple: Loud and full of energy
cards.exampleSentence: The boisterous children ran through the playground.

cards.definition: Anxious or fearful about a future event
cards.definitionSimple: Worried about something that might happen
cards.exampleSentence: He felt apprehensive before the big match.

cards.definition: Cruel, harsh, and controlling; also uncomfortably heavy
cards.definitionSimple: Very harsh and unfair, or so heavy it feels suffocating
cards.exampleSentence: The oppressive heat made it hard to breathe.

cards.definition: A strong feeling that something bad is about to happen
cards.definitionSimple: A scary feeling that something bad is coming
cards.exampleSentence: She had a sense of foreboding as she entered the dark house.

cards.definition: Causing strong disagreement or argument
cards.definitionSimple: Something that people strongly disagree about
cards.exampleSentence: The new rule was controversial among the students.

cards.definition: Leaving no doubt; completely clear
cards.definitionSimple: Absolutely clear with no room for doubt
cards.exampleSentence: Her answer was unequivocal: "No."

cards.definition: Evoking strong interest or admiration; convincing
cards.definitionSimple: So interesting or convincing that you cannot look away or disagree
cards.exampleSentence: The film was so compelling she watched it twice.

cards.definition: Wildly unreasonable or illogical; ridiculous
cards.definitionSimple: So silly it makes no sense at all
cards.exampleSentence: It was absurd to wear a coat in summer heat.

cards.definition: Making no sense; absurd
cards.definitionSimple: Completely makes no sense
cards.exampleSentence: His nonsensical explanation confused everyone.

cards.definition: Incapable of making mistakes; never wrong
cards.definitionSimple: Always right, never ever wrong
cards.exampleSentence: Nobody is infallible — everyone makes mistakes eventually.

cards.definition: In a way that boldly resists or challenges authority
cards.definitionSimple: In a way that shows you refuse to obey or back down
cards.exampleSentence: He defiantly refused to apologise.

cards.definition: Gradually worn away over time
cards.definitionSimple: Slowly worn away bit by bit
cards.exampleSentence: The cliffs had been eroded by the sea over centuries.

cards.definition: Done alone; existing alone without companions
cards.definitionSimple: Alone, by yourself, with nobody else
cards.exampleSentence: He preferred solitary walks in the woods.

cards.definition: Beyond normal natural explanation; magical or ghostly
cards.definitionSimple: Things that science cannot explain, like ghosts or magic
cards.exampleSentence: The old house was said to have supernatural powers.

cards.definition: Mutual trust and friendship among people
cards.definitionSimple: The warm, friendly feeling between people in a group
cards.exampleSentence: There was great camaraderie among the team.

cards.definition: The quality of being clever, original, and inventive
cards.definitionSimple: Being really clever at finding creative solutions
cards.exampleSentence: The engineer solved the problem with great ingenuity.

cards.definition: Lasting for a long time; continuing
cards.definitionSimple: Something that keeps going for a long time
cards.exampleSentence: Their enduring friendship lasted fifty years.

cards.definition: Using careful, logical analysis to examine things
cards.definitionSimple: Thinking carefully and logically about things
cards.exampleSentence: She had an analytical mind and loved solving puzzles.

cards.definition: Never stopping or getting less intense
cards.definitionSimple: Never stopping, always continuing with full force
cards.exampleSentence: The unrelenting rain lasted all week.

cards.definition: Not easily convinced; having doubts
cards.definitionSimple: Not believing something easily, needing proof
cards.exampleSentence: She was skeptical about his excuse.

cards.definition: Feeling troubled or nervous; anxious
cards.definitionSimple: Feeling nervous, worried, and unable to stay calm
cards.exampleSentence: The long wait made her increasingly agitated.

cards.definition: Concerned with real situations rather than theory
cards.definitionSimple: Useful in real life, not just in ideas
cards.exampleSentence: A raincoat is a practical choice for English weather.

cards.definition: Pieces of clothing
cards.definitionSimple: Items of clothing
cards.exampleSentence: She packed her finest garments for the trip.

cards.definition: Easy to reach, use, or understand
cards.definitionSimple: Easy to get to or use
cards.exampleSentence: The information was clearly written and accessible to all.

cards.definition: The detailed planning and organisation of an activity
cards.definitionSimple: The practical planning and organisation of how to do something
cards.exampleSentence: Organising the school trip involved complex logistics.

cards.definition: Built or created again after being damaged or destroyed
cards.definitionSimple: Built again after being destroyed
cards.exampleSentence: The ancient temple was carefully reconstructed.

cards.definition: Hopeful and confident about the future
cards.definitionSimple: Believing good things will happen
cards.exampleSentence: She was optimistic about passing the exam.

cards.definition: A bird, especially one kept for eggs or meat (e.g. chicken)
cards.definitionSimple: A bird like a chicken or duck
cards.exampleSentence: The farmer kept several different types of fowl.

cards.definition: In a modest way, without drawing attention to oneself
cards.definitionSimple: In a quiet, modest way without showing off
cards.exampleSentence: He unassumingly helped clear the tables after dinner.

cards.definition: Relating to money and how it is managed
cards.definitionSimple: To do with money
cards.exampleSentence: He made a careful financial plan before spending.

cards.definition: Direct and outspoken without being tactful; also not sharp
cards.definitionSimple: Saying things in a very direct way without being careful about feelings
cards.exampleSentence: His blunt comment hurt her feelings.

cards.definition: Requiring immediate attention; urgent
cards.definitionSimple: Something that needs to be done right now
cards.exampleSentence: There is a pressing need to fix the leaking roof.

cards.definition: Not given proper care or attention
cards.definitionSimple: Left without being looked after properly
cards.exampleSentence: The neglected garden was full of weeds.

cards.definition: In a way that is very bad or unsuccessful
cards.definitionSimple: Really badly, in a sad and disappointing way
cards.exampleSentence: He dismally failed the test after not revising.

cards.definition: Richly pleasing to the taste or senses
cards.definitionSimple: Deliciously rich and tasty
cards.exampleSentence: The luscious strawberries were perfectly ripe.

cards.definition: Not persuaded or certain about something
cards.definitionSimple: When you still have doubts and are not sure
cards.exampleSentence: He remained unconvinced by the argument.

cards.definition: Likely to change; not staying the same
cards.definitionSimple: Something that keeps changing
cards.exampleSentence: The weather in England is very variable.

cards.definition: Cannot be trusted or depended on
cards.definitionSimple: Something you cannot count on
cards.exampleSentence: The old car was unreliable and often broke down.

cards.definition: Waiting to be decided or dealt with
cards.definitionSimple: Something not finished yet, still waiting
cards.exampleSentence: The decision is still pending.

cards.definition: Unable to make decisions quickly or clearly
cards.definitionSimple: When you cannot make up your mind
cards.exampleSentence: She was indecisive about which book to choose.

cards.definition: Without the knowledge of someone
cards.definitionSimple: Without someone knowing about it
cards.exampleSentence: Unbeknownst to him, a surprise party was being planned.

cards.definition: Very curious and interested
cards.definitionSimple: Really curious and wanting to find out more
cards.exampleSentence: She was intrigued by the mysterious package.

cards.definition: A feeling of deep sadness or distress
cards.definitionSimple: Very deep sadness
cards.exampleSentence: She felt great sorrow when her dog died.

cards.definition: Elaborately decorated sweet foods or cakes
cards.definitionSimple: Fancy sweets, chocolates, or decorated cakes
cards.exampleSentence: The bakery window was full of beautiful confections.

cards.definition: Requiring immediate action or attention
cards.definitionSimple: Needs to happen right now
cards.exampleSentence: There was an urgent message waiting for him.

cards.definition: Failed to notice or consider; also to look over from above
cards.definitionSimple: Not noticed or missed out accidentally
cards.exampleSentence: An important clue had been overlooked by the detective.

cards.definition: A person who supports or argues for something
cards.definitionSimple: Someone who strongly supports an idea
cards.exampleSentence: She was a strong proponent of healthy eating.

cards.definition: A sum of money for a specific purpose
cards.definitionSimple: Money saved or collected for a special reason
cards.exampleSentence: They set up a fund to help the flood victims.

cards.definition: A conclusion reached by reasoning from evidence
cards.definitionSimple: Working out something that is not directly said
cards.exampleSentence: From the clues, the detective made an inference.

cards.definition: To improve the quality or value of something
cards.definitionSimple: To make something better
cards.exampleSentence: The lighting enhanced the beauty of the room.

cards.definition: A strong effect or influence on something
cards.definitionSimple: A big effect on something
cards.exampleSentence: The storm had a huge impact on the town.

cards.definition: A short story with a moral lesson, often with animal characters
cards.definitionSimple: A short story that teaches you something, often with animals
cards.exampleSentence: Aesop's fable about the tortoise and the hare teaches us about patience.

cards.definition: Pleasure gained from getting what you wanted
cards.definitionSimple: The good feeling you get when something you wanted happens
cards.exampleSentence: Finishing the puzzle gave him great gratification.

cards.definition: Walked slowly and heavily, as if very tired
cards.definitionSimple: Walked in a slow, tired, heavy way
cards.exampleSentence: She trudged home through the snow.

cards.definition: A feeling of sorrow for someone else's misfortune
cards.definitionSimple: Feeling sad because something bad happened to someone else
cards.exampleSentence: She felt pity for the stray cat in the rain.

cards.definition: To have a strong effect or meaning for someone
cards.definitionSimple: When something connects with you and feels meaningful
cards.exampleSentence: The story resonated with everyone in the room.

cards.definition: In an extremely unpleasant or annoying way
cards.definitionSimple: In a way that is really annoying and hard to ignore
cards.exampleSentence: He laughed obnoxiously during the film.

cards.definition: Lacking variety; always the same
cards.definitionSimple: Always exactly the same, boring and repetitive
cards.exampleSentence: The unvaried menu made mealtimes dull.

cards.definition: To give someone greater knowledge or understanding
cards.definitionSimple: To help someone understand something better
cards.exampleSentence: The documentary enlightened viewers about climate change.

cards.definition: Amusement or laughter
cards.definitionSimple: Happy laughter and amusement
cards.exampleSentence: The comedy show was a source of great mirth.

cards.definition: Having a run or ladder in (a stocking or tights)
cards.definitionSimple: When tights or stockings get a long rip in them
cards.exampleSentence: She noticed her tights were laddered before the performance.

cards.definition: To put forward an idea or plan for consideration
cards.definitionSimple: To suggest an idea for others to think about
cards.exampleSentence: She proposed a new way to organise the classroom.

cards.definition: Relating to the home and the people in it
cards.definitionSimple: To do with your home and family
cards.exampleSentence: Washing up is a common household chore.

cards.definition: Comparing two things using 'like' or 'as'
cards.example: Her eyes were like stars in the night sky.

cards.definition: Describing something as if it actually is something else (without using like or as)
cards.example: The classroom was a zoo after lunch.

cards.definition: Giving human feelings or actions to non-human things
cards.example: The wind whispered through the trees.

cards.definition: Extreme exaggeration used for effect
cards.example: I've told you a million times!

cards.definition: A word that sounds like what it describes
cards.example: The bees buzzed around the flower.

cards.definition: Placing two contrasting things close together to highlight the difference
cards.example: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.

cards.definition: Deliberately repeating a word or phrase for emphasis
cards.example: We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds.

cards.definition: Explaining something by comparing it to something more familiar
cards.example: The brain is like a computer — it processes information.

cards.definition: The pattern of rhymes at the end of lines in a poem, shown with letters (e.g. ABAB, AABB)
cards.example: 'Roses are red (A), violets are blue (B), sugar is sweet (A), and so are you (B)' — ABAB

cards.definition: Repetition of consonant sounds within or at the end of words
cards.example: The black silk slacks were slick.

cards.definition: A short story with animal characters that teaches a moral lesson
cards.example: The Tortoise and the Hare teaches us that slow and steady wins the race.

cards.definition: Repetition of the same consonant sound at the start of nearby words
cards.example: Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers.

cards.definition: A phrase whose meaning is different from the literal words
cards.example: It's raining cats and dogs.

cards.definition: A technique used by a writer to convey meaning, create effect, or engage the reader
cards.example: "The angry clouds growled overhead." — identify the device (Personification)

cards.definition: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea
A word that expresses an action or state of being
A word that describes or modifies a noun
A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb
Words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings
Words that are spelled and sound the same but have different meanings
Words spelled the same but with different sounds and meanings
A word used in place of a noun
A word that shows the position or relationship between things

cards.question: How do you find a percentage of a number?
cards.method: Find 10% (÷10), find 1% (÷100), then build the percentage you need from those parts
steps: Find 10% by dividing by 10
Find 1% by dividing by 100
Combine to get the percentage you need

cards.question: What is the sum and difference method?
cards.method: Add the two equations to find 2A, then divide by 2 to find A; subtract equations to find 2B, then divide by 2 to find B
steps: Add both equations: (A+B)+(A-B)=2A
Divide by 2 to find A
Subtract equations: (A+B)-(A-B)=2B
Divide by 2 to find B
cards.example: A+B=12, A-B=4. Adding: 2A=16, A=8. Subtracting: 2B=8, B=4.

cards.question: How do you check a division answer?
cards.method: Multiply your answer by the divisor — the result should equal the original number (plus the remainder if there is one)
steps: Multiply answer × divisor
Add remainder if there is one
Check it equals the original number

cards.question: How do you compare decimals using place value?
cards.method: Line up the decimal points, compare digit by digit from left to right
steps: Line up the decimal points
Compare the digits in each column from left to right
The first column where they differ determines which is larger
cards.example: 0.75 vs 0.8: tenths column — 7 vs 8 — so 0.8 is larger

cards.question: How do you find a point on a number line?
cards.method: Count the intervals, find the difference between endpoints, divide to get the step value, then count from the start
steps: Count the total number of intervals between endpoints
Find the difference between start and end values
Divide the difference by number of intervals to get step size
Count intervals from the start to your point and multiply by step size
cards.example: Number line from 0 to 50 with 5 intervals: each step = 10. Point at 3rd mark = 30.

cards.question: How do you write a remainder as a fraction?
cards.method: Put the remainder over the divisor
steps: Find the remainder
Write remainder ÷ divisor as a fraction
Simplify if possible
cards.example: 23 ÷ 4 = 5 remainder 3 → 5 and 3/4

cards.question: How do you do long division?
cards.method: Divide, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down — repeat
steps: Divide: how many times does the divisor go into the current number?
Multiply: write the result
Subtract: find the remainder
Bring down: bring the next digit down and repeat
cards.example: 156 ÷ 4: 4 into 15 = 3 r3, bring down 6 → 36 ÷ 4 = 9. Answer: 39

cards.question: How do you find the area of a rectangle?
steps: Identify the length
Identify the width
Multiply them together
cards.example: Rectangle 8cm × 5cm: area = 40cm²

cards.question: How do you work with ratios?
cards.method: Find the value of one part by dividing the total by the number of parts, then multiply for each share
steps: Add up all the parts of the ratio
Divide the total by this number to find one part
Multiply one part by each ratio number
cards.example: Ratio 3:2, total=25. One part=25÷5=5. Shares: 3×5=15 and 2×5=10

cards.question: What is the Process of Elimination technique?
cards.answer: Read all options, cross out clearly wrong ones, then choose from what remains
options: Read all options, cross out clearly wrong ones, then choose from what remains
Choose the first one that seems right

cards.question: What should you do BEFORE answering comprehension questions?
cards.answer: Read the entire passage first, then read the questions
options: Read the questions first to know what to look for
Read the passage and questions at the same time
Answer what you can remember without re-reading
Read the entire passage first, then read the questions

cards.question: How do you find evidence to support a comprehension answer?
cards.answer: Go back to the passage and underline or note the exact words that support your answer
options: Underline evidence in the passage text
Go back to the passage and underline or note the exact words that support your answer

cards.question: What should you do before selecting a final answer?
cards.answer: Re-read the question carefully to make sure you are answering exactly what was asked
options: Re-read the question carefully to make sure you are answering exactly what was asked
Pick the answer you chose first

cards.question: How should you use spare time at the end of an exam?
cards.answer: Return to the 3 questions you felt least confident about and check your answers
options: Return to the 3 questions you felt least confident about and check your answers
Read the passage again from the start
